title: Gammon with parsley sauce
subtitle: Boiled gammon steak with a sharp, herb-rich white sauce
excerpt: Gammon steaks simmered briefly and served with a classic British parsley sauce: a béchamel with enough fresh flat-leaf parsley to make it properly green and sharp. Simple, fast, and very satisfying.
sourceNotes: Parsley sauce with boiled gammon or bacon is a classic of the British culinary tradition. Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes parsley sauce as a standard British sauce alongside boiled ham and bacon. The combination is standard in British and Irish domestic cooking through the twentieth century.

content.text: Parsley sauce is one of the oldest and best British sauces: a plain béchamel carrying enough chopped flat-leaf parsley to turn it green and give it real flavour. The parsley must go in at the last moment, after the heat is off, to keep its colour bright and its flavour fresh. A parsley sauce added too early turns khaki and tastes of cooked leaves rather than herb.

Gammon steaks are available in most supermarkets and need only 8 to 10 minutes of gentle cooking. They can be simmered in water or a mixture of water and milk, which softens the salt slightly. The parsley sauce is made separately and takes about 10 minutes.

Place {{gammon-steak}} gammon steaks in a saucepan and cover with cold water. Add the bay leaves and peppercorns. Bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until cooked through. Lift out and keep warm.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a saucepan over medium heat. Add {{plain-flour}} of flour and stir continuously for 2 minutes to cook out the raw flavour. Gradually add {{whole-milk}} of milk, a splash at a time, whisking constantly to prevent lumps. Once all the milk is incorporated, cook, stirring, for 5 minutes until the sauce is thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. Season with salt and pepper.

Remove from the heat. Stir in {{parsley-flat}} of finely chopped flat-leaf parsley and {{lemon-juice}} of lemon juice. The sauce should turn distinctly green. Taste and adjust — it should be sharp, herby, and creamy in balance. Pour generously over the gammon steaks. Serve with mashed potato or boiled new potatoes.

items.symptom: Sauce has turned khaki rather than green
items.cause: Parsley was added while the sauce was still on the heat
items.fix: Always add the parsley off the heat and serve immediately. If colour is lost, the flavour will also be diminished — remake the sauce.

items.cause: Milk was added too quickly without whisking
items.fix: Add milk in small amounts and whisk between each addition until smooth. Lumps at this stage can usually be beaten out over gentle heat.
Soak gammon steaks in cold water for 1 hour before cooking. The parsley sauce should not add any additional salt if the gammon is already salty.

items.cause: Not enough flour, or insufficient cooking time
items.fix: Cook the sauce over medium heat for a further 3 to 5 minutes, stirring constantly. It will thicken further with time.

content.text: Parsley sauce is the most British of the white sauces, and gammon is among its most natural partners. The combination appears in Beeton and in nearly every significant British cookery book of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, which gives some indication of how settled and accepted it is. Parsley sauce with boiled ham or bacon is a dish that exists without controversy in the British culinary tradition: it is what you make, everyone knows how to make it, and it is rarely described or celebrated because it does not need to be. Its interest lies in the quality of execution rather than the novelty of the concept.
